content:
6 min read
Just now

--

AI can scale marketing output. It cannot replace the creative judgment that makes people care.

The AI Marketing Conversation Is Growing Up
The first phase of AI in marketing was dominated by production. AI could create more assets, generate campaign variations, translate copy, adapt content for channels, and reduce the manual work that slows down modern marketing teams.
That phase is still underway. It is also becoming less interesting.
The more important question now is what happens after those capabilities become normal. If every brand can generate more content, the ability to generate more content becomes less distinctive. If every agency can promise faster production, speed becomes the baseline. If every platform can produce fluent copy and polished images, fluency and polish stop carrying the same value.
The next marketing question is not simply how much AI can make. It is what remains valuable when nearly everything can be made.
Recent Axios coverage from Cannes captured that shift. Media and branding leaders argued that AI cannot manufacture “something real,” even as it floods the market with generated content. Marketing leaders also argued that AI should give creativity more room rather than replace the human judgment that gives creative work its force.
Those two ideas belong together. They describe the emerging authenticity premium.
AI Can Produce the Asset, Not the Reality Behind It
AI can produce many useful things. It can draft, summarize, translate, visualize, adapt, and scale. For global brands, those capabilities are practical. A company that spends heavily on video translation, copy adaptation, pricing communication, campaign localization, or production coordination has every reason to explore AI tools that remove operational drag.
That is not hype. It is workflow reality.
But marketing has never been valuable only because an asset exists. The value comes from what the asset carries. A brand message carries history, positioning, tone, risk, and the relationship between a company and its audience. A campaign carries choices about timing, culture, audience, and credibility. A piece of media carries reporting, access, editorial standards, and trust.
AI can imitate many surfaces of communication. It cannot create the underlying reality that makes communication credible.
That is the core problem for brands. A synthetic image can look expensive and still feel empty. A generated campaign line can sound fluent and still feel generic. A personalized message can be technically relevant and still feel intrusive or false. More content does not automatically create more meaning.
As AI-generated material becomes ordinary, audiences will become more sensitive to work that has no weight behind it. They may not always know which tools were used, but they can often sense when the work feels disconnected from real judgment.
Authenticity Becomes a Business Asset
Authenticity is often used lazily in marketing. It can become a vague word for warmth, relatability, or brand purpose. AI forces a stricter definition.
Authenticity is the value created when work feels connected to real judgment, real experience, real accountability, and a credible brand position. It does not require every asset to be manually produced by humans. It does require that the work has a human center strong enough to prevent AI from flattening the brand into generic competence.
That is where the premium appears.
AI expands supply. It does not expand attention at the same rate. The result is a market where more production can easily become more noise. The brands that win will not simply be the brands that publish more. They will be the brands whose work still feels distinctive, grounded, and worth believing.
This has direct implications for agencies. If AI-enabled production becomes available to everyone, agencies cannot defend their value by promising volume alone. They will need to show how they protect the brand from sameness, how they govern synthetic content, how they preserve creative judgment, and how they decide where human oversight is essential.
The agency question moves from output capacity to operating discipline.
Creativity Does Not Disappear
The common fear is that AI will replace creative people. That fear is understandable, but the framing is too narrow.
AI will change creative work. Some tasks will be automated. Some production processes will be compressed. Some roles will be redefined. Parts of the creative workflow that once required significant labor will become faster and cheaper.
But AI will not become valuable in marketing without creativity.
Creativity is not only the act of making the asset. It is the ability to understand the problem, find the tension, recognize the audience, protect the brand, and decide which ideas deserve to move forward. It is the ability to reject the merely plausible. It is the discipline to know when an output is attractive, efficient, and wrong.
That kind of creativity becomes more important as AI produces more options.
A machine can generate campaign routes, but it cannot independently know which route carries the brand’s history. It can write in a requested tone, but it cannot know whether that tone has been earned. It can create something that looks emotionally polished, but it cannot understand whether the emotion is honest for the brand and the moment.
This is where the role of creative people changes rather than disappears. The work moves upstream into problem definition and downstream into selection, refinement, and accountability. The human contribution becomes less about producing every possible asset and more about governing what the system should create, what it should reject, and what the brand should be willing to stand behind.
The more AI can produce, the more valuable it becomes to know what should not be produced.
The New Agency Discipline
For the major holdcos and their competitors, AI is becoming part of the operating architecture. That will affect cost, speed, staffing, and production models. It will also affect the relationship between clients and agencies.
Clients will expect AI-enabled efficiency. They will expect more speed, more adaptation, and more flexible production. Those expectations will become standard.
The stronger agencies will move beyond that baseline. They will help clients use AI without losing distinctiveness. They will define rules for synthetic assets, protect brand voice, create review standards, and make sure creative judgment does not disappear inside automated workflows.
That requires a different kind of discipline. AI can make agencies faster, but it can also make every agency sound more alike. It can reduce waste, but it can also reduce originality if the system is not governed by people with taste, experience, and strategic judgment.
The agency of the AI era cannot simply be a production machine. It has to be a decision system.
What Serious Marketers Should Watch
The authenticity premium will show up in several practical ways.
Brands will need clearer rules for when synthetic content is acceptable and when it creates reputational risk. Agencies will need stronger processes for reviewing AI-assisted work before it reaches the market. Creative leaders will need to defend the value of judgment, not only the value of craft. Executives will need to understand that AI adoption is not only a technology question. It is also a brand governance question.
The core test is simple.
Does the work still feel connected to something real?
That question will become more important as AI becomes less visible inside marketing workflows. The risk is not only that audiences discover an asset was generated. The deeper risk is that the brand becomes more efficient while becoming less believable.
AI can help marketing teams move faster. It can reduce cost. It can expand creative exploration. It can remove tedious work that consumed time and budget.
It cannot create meaning on its own.
When everyone can generate, the advantage moves to the brands and agencies that still know how to make the work matter.
